<argument>
The title’s “gift” is not mainly social reform; it is Earth’s capitalist system, wrapped as benevolence. Once Earth returns, <quote>“Immediately, the Earthmen established what they called ‘corporations’”</quote> and explicitly, <quote>“The object of the visit was trade.”</quote> That is the core import.

Earth then seduces Zur with the language of free gifts while imposing freight, interest, mortgages, and partial ownership: <quote>“Every item is given to you absolutely free... All we ask is that you pay the freight charges”</quote> and then <quote>“I offer you credit!”</quote> This “gift” is consumer capitalism—advertising, installment debt, and corporate control. The result is planetary buyout: <quote>“We have bought you out.”</quote>

By contrast, destruction of the caste system is only a late explanatory side effect. Broderick says, after the takeover is complete, <quote>“That’s why we had to break down your caste system.”</quote> The wording matters: they “had to” break it down in order to complete the broader economic project. So the story’s “gift from Earth” is capitalism itself, not merely caste reform.
</argument>
